engine.maia: Maia-1 rating-band networks 1100-1900 via zerofish WASM lc0, nodes=1

accuracy.method: mean of volatility-weighted mean and harmonic mean of per-move accuracies (Lichess formulas)

mistakes.title: Pushed …g5 into the h4-pawn's teeth — with a bishop watching from c1
mistakes.explanation: You were more than three pawns better here, and one push gave nearly all of it back. The g5-square is attacked twice — by the h4-pawn and, through the empty dark squares, by the bishop sitting all the way back on c1 — and after 22.hxg5 no recapture works: 22…fxg5 runs into 23.Bxg5! hitting d8 and h6 at once, and even the best retreat 22…Ng8 23.gxf6 Nxf6 leaves White's pieces suddenly breathing. Your play was on the other wing anyway: 21…c4! pries open the center where your Nd4, Bd6 and rooks all point, and after 22.Be3 cxd3 23.cxd3 Nhf5 the engine keeps you at +3.4 with White's king still stuck in the middle.

takeaways.lesson: Before pushing a pawn, count every attacker of its landing square — including bishops on their home squares.
takeaways.detail: A bishop on c1 or f8 that has never moved still owns its whole diagonal the moment it opens. Trace the diagonal of the square you're pushing to, all the way to the edge, before committing the pawn — a pawn can never step back.

takeaways.lesson: Attack where your pieces point, not where the opponent's do.
takeaways.detail: Your knight, bishop and both rooks aimed at the center and queenside; White's h4-pawn and h3-rook owned the kingside. When you're winning, open lines on your strong wing (…c4 here) and keep the opponent's wing closed.

drillLinks.label: When to push the pawns in front of the king — discussion

mistakes.title: The automatic recapture opened the diagonal that forked you
mistakes.explanation: 22…fxg5 looks like the move that restores material — but watch what the f6-pawn's disappearance does: the diagonal from g5 to d8 is suddenly empty, and 23.Bxg5! attacks your rook on d8 and your knight on h6 in the same breath. Neither can save the other: you chose 23…Rdg8 and 24.Bxh6 took the knight, turning a game you still stood better in into a lost one. The engine's 22…Ng8 admits the g-pawn is gone, and after 23.gxf6 Nxf6 the knight returns to a real square and you are still the one pressing (+0.60). A recaptured pawn is worth much less than a knight.

takeaways.lesson: When a recapture feels automatic, that is exactly the moment to stop and check it.
takeaways.detail: Forced-looking moves are where blunders hide, because you calculate them the least. Before any recapture, do one scan: what lines does my capturing pawn or piece leave behind, and what lands on them?

takeaways.lesson: Every pawn capture opens two lines — yours and theirs.
takeaways.detail: Here f6xg5 opened g5–d8 for White's bishop before you could use anything it opened for you. Make it a habit to look along the file and diagonal a capturing pawn vacates, from the opponent's side of the board.

drillLinks.label: Capture the defender — Lichess puzzle theme

mistakes.title: Traded rooks on a square the sleeping bishop could see
mistakes.explanation: 30…Rg5 offers a trade, but count the pieces looking at g5: behind White's knight on e3 stands the bishop on d2, and d2–g5 is one straight dark-squared line. After 31.Rxg5 Rxg5 the discovered attack was loaded — 32.Nc4! moved the blocker with a threat of its own against your d6-bishop, so there was no tempo to rescue the rook, and 33.Bxg5 won it whole. That's the same c1–h6 diagonal claiming its third victim. 30…Qf7 instead puts a second attacker on the h5-rook and keeps your rooks connected on the g-file; you're worse, but the position still asks White questions.

takeaways.lesson: Before parking a piece on a square, look behind every enemy piece aimed at it — blockers move.
takeaways.detail: A bishop or rook 'blocked' by its own knight is a discovered attack waiting for one tempo. Scan each enemy line piece, extend its line through friendly blockers, and treat any square on that extended line as contested.

takeaways.lesson: After a proposed trade, ask who owns the square once all the captures finish.
takeaways.detail: Play the full sequence in your head to the final recapture, then count attackers and defenders of that square one more time. Here the sequence ended with your rook on g5 — attacked by a bishop, defended by nothing.

mistakes.title: Took the bishop that was bait for a royal fork
mistakes.explanation: 38.Bf6 walks the bishop to a square your queen attacks — and that generosity should have set off alarms. The e3-knight was one hop from d5, and from d5 it touches both c7 and f6: 38…Qxf6 39.Nd5+! forked your king and queen, and after 39…Kd7 40.Nxf6+ the queen was gone for a bishop. When an engine hangs a piece to your queen, the piece is never hanging — ask what recapture, check or fork the capture allows before touching it. The cold-blooded 38…b4 keeps the queen on e6 guarding the king's neighborhood and makes White prove the win.

takeaways.lesson: Before capturing anything, ask why you're being allowed to take it.
takeaways.detail: A free piece from a stronger opponent is a decoy until proven otherwise. Name the follow-up you'd fear most — a check, a fork, a pin — and look for it concretely before your hand moves.

takeaways.lesson: Scan every knight check before moving your queen.
takeaways.detail: Knight forks against king and queen decide more club games than any other tactic. Each time your queen moves, find every square an enemy knight could check from, and make sure none of them also touches the queen's new square.

mistakes.title: A pawn push on the wrong errand while the mating net closed
mistakes.explanation: Down a queen with your king stranded on e6, only one thing matters: shelter and stubbornness. 41…c4 spends the tempo on a pawn — and invites 42.dxc4, ripping open the d-file right next to your king with White's rook already sitting on d1; from there the engine announces mate in nine, with Qb6+ and Rd7 weaving the net. 41…Be7 instead covers f6 and d8, gives the king a flight plan via d7, and forces White to demonstrate technique. You can't save a queen-down position, but you can make every win expensive — and engines aside, human opponents crack in exactly those positions.

takeaways.lesson: When your king is caught in the open, every move must be about the king.
takeaways.detail: No pawn push, no counterplay dream, no material count matters while mate is in the air. Each move, ask first: does this block a check, cover an escape square, or bring a defender closer? If not, discard it.

takeaways.lesson: Never allow a file or diagonal near your exposed king to be opened.
takeaways.detail: Captures travel down open lines. Before any pawn move in a bad position, check whether the reply opens a file toward your king — here 42.dxc4 turned the d-file into the highway for White's d1-rook.

drillLinks.label: Defending against an attack — Lichess blog

mistakes.title: Retreated a monster knight that nobody was attacking
mistakes.explanation: Your knight on d4 was the best piece on the board — centralized, untouchable, cramping everything White wanted to do. 9…Nb5 gave that up voluntarily, and the hole it left was filled at once: 10.Nd5! planted White's knight on the mirror-image square, hitting f6 and eyeing b6 and c7, and you spent the next three moves (…Be6, …Nd6, …Qd7) undoing the damage while your advantage evaporated from +1.86 to +0.12. The move the position wanted was 9…f5!, kicking the e4-bishop with tempo and grabbing even more space — the knight on d4 needed no permission to stay.

takeaways.lesson: Don't retreat a strong piece until the opponent shows you a concrete threat against it.
takeaways.detail: Before moving a well-placed piece, name the exact enemy move that would force it away. If you can't name one, the piece stays and you improve something else — vague discomfort is not a threat.

takeaways.lesson: Look for pawn kicks against enemy pieces before rearranging your own.
takeaways.detail: A pawn move that hits a bishop or knight wins a whole tempo — the opponent must answer. Here 9…f5 attacked the e4-bishop and expanded your center in one move; scan for such kicks every time it's your turn.

drillLinks.label: Opening principles: king-safety-driven development — Lichess blog

highlights.note: Against Stockfish's passive 1.e3 you simply took the full centre with …d5 and …e5 — the principled answer, and the engine's own choice both times.
…Nd4 planted the knight on a supported central square it could never be evicted from, keeping your +2.8 edge — the position was completely won before the later slips.

openingReport.note: Stockfish's 1.e3 has no theory to leave — and you answered it the model way, taking the full centre with …d5 and …e5. The first improvable decision came at 6…Nxd4 (59.3% → 51.1%): recapturing with the e-pawn (6…exd4) keeps the pawn duo and the structure.
